subject: mathematics
prose_summary:
Section 12.1 recalls the decimal place-value system and applies it to convert between fractions and decimals. It reviews that decimals extend the Indian place-value system to tenths, hundredths, thousandths and their sums, reading 27.53 as 2 tens, 7 units, 5 tenths, 3 hundredths. A fraction–decimal game between Jonali and Pallabi and a spice-buying context (50 g, 100 g, 25 g, 250 g expressed in kilograms) lead students to write fractions with denominators 10, 100, 1000 as sums of tenths, hundredths, and thousandths and as decimals. The section notes that multiplying and dividing decimals will be shown to be natural extensions of the procedures already learnt for counting numbers and for fractions. From a pattern in these problems the section derives the rule for dividing a number by a power of ten — write the number with a decimal point at the end, count the zeroes in the divisor, and move the point left by that many places, inserting leading zeroes if needed — illustrated by 123 ÷ 10 = 12.3, 24 ÷ 100 = 0.24, 12 ÷ 1000 = 0.012, and 12345 ÷ 1000 = 12.345.

Section 12.2 applies fraction multiplication to multiply decimals and then reasons out a general placement rule. Worked Examples 1–5 — five pens at ₹9.5, a car covering 12.5 km per litre for 7.5 litres, Ajay's twice-daily walk of 0.827 km for six days, the area of a 5.7 cm × 13.3 cm rectangle, and 5.8 × 1.24 — each convert the decimals to fractions over powers of ten, multiply numerators and denominators, and read off the decimal product. Tabulating the number of digits after the decimal point in the multiplier, multiplicand, and product, the section frames the rule: multiply the numbers with the decimal points removed, then place the point so that the number of decimal places equals the sum of the decimal places in the two factors (so 596 × 248 = 147808 gives 5.96 × 24.8 = 147.808). It then examines when a product is greater or less than the numbers multiplied, tabulating three situations — both numbers greater than 1, both between 0 and 1, and one of each — mirroring the behaviour of fractions. Its Figure it Out set applies decimal multiplication to cloth, stationery, coin-stack height, orange prices, wholesale profit, and sign-of-size reasoning about which products are less than 1.

Section 12.3 applies several division procedures and reasons about terminating and non-terminating quotients. It divides decimals by powers of ten by moving the point left (3.9 ÷ 10 = 0.39, 3.9 ÷ 100 = 0.039), then converts fractions to equivalent fractions with denominators 10, 100, 1000 (29 ÷ 2 = 14.5, 29 ÷ 4 = 7.25). Where no such equivalent fraction exists, it develops division using place value — 'long division' — regrouping thousands into hundreds, ones into tenths, tenths into hundredths, and so on, worked through 1324 ÷ 4 = 331, 1325 ÷ 4 = 331.25, 237 ÷ 8 = 29.625, a decimal dividend 9.5 ÷ 4 = 2.375, and 0.06 ÷ 5 = 0.012, always placing the decimal point in the quotient when ones are first regrouped into tenths. For a decimal divisor it multiplies dividend and divisor by the same power of ten to make the divisor a whole number (126 ÷ 2.5 = 50.4; 4.68 ÷ 0.13 = 468 ÷ 13). It then investigates 10 ÷ 3 = 3.333…, which never ends because the remainder is always 1, and 1 ÷ 7 = 0.142857…, whose remainders and quotient digits repeat in a cycle, leading to the 'magic number' 142857 and Emil Artin's still-unsolved 1927 conjecture about cyclic numbers, before relating the dividend, divisor, and quotient when the divisor is a decimal.

Section 12.4 applies decimal multiplication to the leap-year problem and reasons through successive calendar corrections. Since the Earth takes 365.2422 days to orbit the Sun, 0.2422 × 100 = 24.22 extra days accumulate over a century, motivating the leap-year rule of adding a day every fourth year. Using divisibility decision charts (divisible by 4? by 100? by 400?), the section builds expressions for the number of calendar days over 100 and 1000 years — for example (24 × 366) + (76 × 365) = 36,524 days for a century once the 100th year is excluded, and a four-term sum for a millennium once the 400th year is restored — and compares them with the actual 100 × 365.2422 = 36,524.22 and 1000 × 365.2422 = 3,65,242.2 days, showing how the every-4, skip-100, keep-400 scheme progressively narrows the gap. It also observes, through a table of 1/2, 1/(2×2), … and 1/5, 1/(5×5), …, that fractions whose denominators are built only from 2s and 5s give terminating decimals. Its Figure it Out set applies decimal arithmetic to unit-price comparison, number-line reading, profit, shelf-packing, unit conversions, a Patiganita problem of Sridharacharya, fill-in-the-boxes products, and quotient patterns.

enumerated_worked_examples.description: A car travels 12.5 km per litre; distance on 7.5 litres = 12.5 × 7.5 = (125×75)/(10×10) = 9375/100 = 93.75 km.
Ajay walks 827 m (0.827 km) each way to school, twice a day, 6 days a week: 0.827 × 2 = 1.654 km/day, and 1.654 × 6 = 9.924 km/week.
Area of a rectangle with sides 5.7 cm and 13.3 cm = 5.7 × 13.3 = (57×133)/100 = 7581/100 = 75.81 sq cm.
Find 5.8 × 1.24 using the rule: 58 × 124 = 7192, and with 1 + 2 = 3 decimal places the product is 7.192.
Anuja cuts a 3.9 m ribbon into 10 equal pieces: 3.9 ÷ 10 = (39/10) × (1/10) = 39/100 = 0.39 m each (and into 100 pieces gives 0.039 m).
Find 1324 ÷ 4 by division using place value (long division), regrouping thousands into hundreds, etc.: 1324 ÷ 4 = 331.
Find 237 ÷ 8 by long division with a decimal quotient, regrouping ones into tenths, tenths into hundredths, hundredths into thousandths: 237 ÷ 8 = 29.625.
A shopkeeper packs 9.5 kg of sugar equally into 4 bags: 9.5 ÷ 4 = 2.375 kg per bag, placing the decimal point before dividing the tenths.
Find 0.06 ÷ 5 by place value: 0 ones, 0 tenths, 6 hundredths give 1 hundredth with remainder regrouped into thousandths, so 0.06 ÷ 5 = 0.012.
Ravi covers 126 km in 2.5 hours; average speed = 126 ÷ 2.5. Converting the divisor: 126 × (10/25) = 1260/25 = 50.4 km/h.
Find 4.68 ÷ 1.3 and 4.68 ÷ 0.13 by making the divisor a whole number: multiply dividend and divisor by the same power of ten, so 4.68 ÷ 0.13 = 468 ÷ 13.

enumerated_exercises.description: Find the products in tenths, hundredths, etc.: (a) 6 × 4 tenths; (b) 7 × 0.3; (c) 9 × 5 hundredths.

Figure it Out Q3, section 12.2 p.73
enumerated_exercises.description: Thejus needs 1.65 m of cloth for a shirt; find the cloth needed for 3 shirts.

enumerated_exercises.description: Meenu buys 4 notebooks at ₹15.50 and 3 erasers at ₹2.75 each; find the total spent.

enumerated_exercises.description: A rupee coin is 1.45 mm thick; find the total height in centimetres of a stack of 36 coins.

enumerated_exercises.description: Oranges cost ₹56.50 per kg; find the price of 2.250 kg, and decide whether writing 56.50 as 56.5 and 2.250 as 2.25 gives the same product and why.

enumerated_exercises.description: Dwarakanath buys notebooks at ₹23.6 wholesale and sells at ₹30 each; find the profit on selling 50 books in a week.

Figure it Out Q9, section 12.2 p.73
enumerated_exercises.description: Without multiplying, decide which products are less than 1: (a) 7 × 0.6; (b) 0.7 × 0.6; (c) 0.7 × 6; (d) 0.07 × 0.06.

enumerated_exercises.description: Complete a table multiplying 5.7, 23.02, 0.92, 0.306, and 24.67 by 10, 100, and 1000.
